explanation: -(으)ㄹ걸(요) expresses the speaker's assumption or guess about a present or past situation — like saying 'probably would' or 'must have.' It can also carry a tone of regret when reflecting on the past, similar to 'I should have.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ걸(요)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ걸(요) directly to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 지금쯤 집에 갔을걸요 — They must have gone home by now.
버스가 곧 올걸요 — The bus will probably come soon.
그때 여기서 살걸 — I should have lived here back then.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 을걸(요)
rules.description: Attach 을걸(요) to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 벌써 다 먹었을걸요 — They've probably already eaten it all.
그 책을 읽을걸 — I probably should have read that book.

notes.title: Past-tense anchor
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ걸 often sits inside an past-tense clause (갔을걸, 먹었을걸) to speculate about something that already happened.

notes.title: Regret tone
notes.text: When used with a plain verb stem and no past marker inside, it can sound like wistful regret: 일찍 갈걸 ('I should have left early').

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also ID 285 -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 for another way to express strong assumptions.

explanation: -나 마나 means 'whether or not' in the sense that the outcome is the same either way. It carries a resigned or dismissive nuance — the action or state barely matters because the result will not change.

rules.title: Verb → + 나 마나
rules.description: Attach 나 마나 directly to the verb stem regardless of the final consonant.

examples.english: 가나 마나 똑같을 거예요 — Whether I go or not, it'll be the same.
먹나 마나 맛이 없어요 — Whether I eat it or not, it tastes bad.
하나 마나 결과는 같아요 — Whether I do it or not, the result is the same.

rules.title: Adjective without 받침 → + 나 마나
rules.description: For adjectives ending in a vowel, attach 나 마나 directly.

examples.english: 크나 마나 다 비싸요 — Whether it's big or not, it's all expensive.

rules.title: Adjective with 받침 → + 으나 마나
rules.description: For adjectives ending in a consonant, attach 으나 마나.

examples.english: 작으나 마나 쓸 만해요 — Whether it's small or not, it's usable.
많으나 마나 필요 없어요 — Whether there's a lot or not, I don't need it.

notes.title: Negative context
notes.text: -나 마나 almost always appears in negative or hopeless contexts. Saying 가나 마나 기쁠 거예요 sounds unnatural because the result is positive.

notes.title: Fixed expression
notes.text: The second half 마나 never changes. Only the first half conjugates to match the verb or adjective.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 means 'no way' or 'there's no reason to.' It expresses strong disbelief that something is possible. The literal sense is 'there is no logic/reason for that to happen,' making it firmer than a simple guess.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 리가 없다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 사람이 거기에 갈 리가 없어요 — There's no way that person would go there.
그가 그런 실수를 할 리가 없어요 — There's no way he would make such a mistake.
그 집에 살 리가 없어요 — There's no way they live in that house.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 을 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 리가 없다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 아이가 이걸 먹을 리가 없어요 — There's no way the child would eat this.
그 책을 읽을 리가 없어요 — There's no way he read that book.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: This grammar pairs naturally with words like 거짓말, 실수, 그런 일 — things that are hard to believe.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also ID 281 -(으)ㄹ걸(요) for supposition, and ID 197 -(으)ㄴ/는 대로 for immediate action.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 척하다 means 'pretend to have done' something. The past modifier -(으)ㄴ attaches to the verb, and 척하다 means 'to pretend.' Use this when someone acts as if a past or completed action is true — even when it is not.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 척하다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 척하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 친구가 간 척했어요 — My friend pretended to have gone.
저를 모른 척했어요 — He pretended not to know me.
아이가 잔 척했어요 — The child pretended to have slept.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 은 척하다
rules.description: Attach 은 척하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 다 먹은 척했어요 — She pretended to have eaten it all.
못 들은 척했어요 — He pretended not to have heard.

notes.title: Most common usage
notes.text: 모른 척하다 is by far the most frequent collocation you will hear in daily conversation.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 288 for the more formal equivalent -(으)ㄴ 체하다, and ID 289 for pretending an action is currently happening.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 체하다 means 'pretend to have done' and is functionally identical to -(으)ㄴ 척하다. The difference is tone: 체하다 is more formal, literary, and slightly old-fashioned, while 척하다 is common in everyday speech.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 체하다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 체하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 아무것도 모른 체했어요 — She pretended to know nothing at all.
잔 체하고 조용히 나갔어요 — He pretended to be asleep and quietly went out.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 은 체하다
rules.description: Attach 은 체하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 이미 먹은 체했어요 — He pretended he had already eaten.
못 들은 체하며 고개를 돌렸어요 — She turned her head, pretending not to have heard.

notes.title: Formal and written
notes.text: Use 체하다 in novels, news articles, or formal essays. In spoken Korean, 척하다 sounds far more natural.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 287 -(으)ㄴ 척하다 for the everyday equivalent, and ID 289 for pretending an ongoing action.

explanation: -는 척하다 means 'pretend to be doing.' The present modifier -는 attaches to the verb, so this form describes faking an action that appears to be in progress at that moment.

rules.title: All verbs → + 는 척하다
rules.description: Attach 는 척하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 아이가 자는 척했어요 — The child pretended to be sleeping.
공부하는 척했어요 — He pretended to be studying.
선생님 말씀을 듣는 척했어요 — She pretended to be listening to the teacher.

notes.title: Present vs past pretending
notes.text: Use -는 척하다 when the action looks ongoing, and -(으)ㄴ 척하다 (ID 287) when the action looks already completed.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: 자는 척하다, 공부하는 척하다, and 듣는 척하다 are extremely common in daily conversation.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사는 척하다, 알다 → 아는 척하다

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)다고 하다 is indirect speech for action verbs. It reports what someone said they do, are doing, or did. Think of it as wrapping the plain speech form -(ㄴ/는)다 inside 다고 하다 to say 'they said that they do.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ다고 하다
rules.description: Action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ take ㄴ다고 하다 in the present tense.

examples.english: 친구가 내일 온다고 해요 — My friend says he's coming tomorrow.
엄마가 금방 오신다고 하셨어요 — Mom said she'll be here soon.
그 사람이 여기 산다고 했어요 — He said he lives here.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 는다고 하다
rules.description: Action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ take 는다고 하다 in the present tense.

examples.english: 동생이 밥을 먹는다고 했어요 — My younger sibling said he's eating.
그녀가 책을 읽는다고 해요 — She says she's reading a book.
선생님이 열심히 공부한다고 하셨어요 — The teacher said she studies hard.

notes.title: Tense shifts inside
notes.text: You can embed past or future tense inside: 갔다고 했어요 ('said he went'), 갈 거라고 했어요 ('said he will go').

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 291 for adjectives, ID 292 for nouns, and IDs 293–295 for other speech acts like suggestions and commands.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.example: 살다 → 산다고 하다, 알다 → 안다고 하다

explanation: -다고 하다 is indirect speech for adjectives. It reports what someone said about a state or quality — 'they said that it is [adjective].' All adjectives use the same -다고 attachment with no 받침 variation.

rules.title: All adjectives → + 다고 하다
rules.description: Attach 다고 하다 directly to any adjective stem. There is no 받침 variation for adjectives.

examples.english: 이 식당이 맛있다고 해요 — They say this restaurant is delicious.
오늘 바쁘다고 했어요 — She said she's busy today.
그 집이 예쁘다고 해요 — They say that house is pretty.

notes.title: 아니다 is special
notes.text: Although 아니다 is grammatically an adjective, it does not take 다고. It becomes 아니라고 하다, matching the noun pattern in ID 292.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 290 for action verbs, ID 292 for nouns, and IDs 296–300 for echo and follow-up forms.

explanation: -(이)라고 하다 is indirect speech for nouns. It reports what someone said something is — 'they said it is [noun].' Use 이라고 after a noun with 받침, and 라고 after a noun without 받침.

rules.title: Noun without 받침 → + 라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 라고 하다 to nouns ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 그 사람이 의사라고 해요 — They say that person is a doctor.
한국 학생이라고 했어요 — She said she's a Korean student.

rules.title: Noun with 받침 → + 이라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 이라고 하다 to nouns ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 우리 선생님이라고 했어요 — He said he's our teacher.
큰 회사의 회사원이라고 해요 — They say he works at a big company.

notes.title: 아니다 follows this pattern
notes.text: 아니라고 하다 behaves like a noun: it always uses 라고, never 다고.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 290 for action verbs, ID 291 for adjectives, and IDs 293–295 for commands, suggestions, and questions.

explanation: -자고 하다 reports a suggestion or proposal. It wraps the propositive ending -자 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone suggested that we do something.'

rules.title: All verbs → + 자고 하다
rules.description: Attach 자고 하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 친구가 영화관에 가자고 해요 — My friend suggests going to the cinema.
저녁을 먹자고 했어요 — He suggested having dinner.
숙제를 내일 하자고 해요 — She suggests doing the homework tomorrow.

notes.title: Softened command
notes.text: -자고 하다 is less forceful than -(으)라고 하다 (ID 294). It always includes the speaker in the proposed action, like saying 'let's.'

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 294 for commands and ID 295 for questions.

explanation: -(으)라고 하다 reports a command, order, or strong request. It wraps the imperative ending -(으)라 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone told someone else to do something.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 라고 하다 directly to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 빨리 가라고 했어요 — He told me to go quickly.
여기로 오라고 하셨어요 — She told me to come here.
조용히 살라고 했어요 — They told me to live quietly.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 으라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 으라고 하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 약을 먹으라고 했어요 — The doctor told me to take medicine.
이 책을 읽으라고 하셨어요 — The teacher told me to read this book.

notes.title: Not for self
notes.text: -(으)라고 하다 is for commands given to someone else. If the subject is telling themselves, use -(으)려고 하다 or a different structure.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 293 for suggestions (-자고 하다) and ID 295 for questions (-냐고 하다).

explanation: -냐고 하다 reports a question. It wraps the plain interrogative ending -냐 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone asked if...' Use this for yes/no questions and wh-questions alike.

rules.title: All verbs → + 냐고 하다
rules.description: Attach 냐고 하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 어디 가냐고 물어봤어요 — He asked where I was going.
밥 먹었냐고 했어요 — He asked if I had eaten.
뭐 하냐고 해요 — He asks what I'm doing.

notes.title: Replace the question mark
notes.text: Take the original question, drop the question mark, and attach 냐고: 갔어? → 갔냐고, 뭐 해? → 뭐 하냐고.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–294 for statements, suggestions, and commands.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사냐고 하다, 알다 → 아냐고 하다

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? is an echo question. It repeats what someone just said to seek confirmation or express mild surprise — like saying 'You mean...?' or 'So you're saying...?' Adding 요 softens the tone.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다고(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다고(요)?.

examples.english: 내일 간다고요? — You mean you're going tomorrow?
혼자 온다고? — You're coming alone?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다고(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다고(요)?.

examples.english: 그걸 먹는다고요? — You're going to eat that?
혼자 한다고요? — You mean you're doing it alone?

rules.title: Adjective → + 다고(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다고(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 맛있다고요? — You mean it's delicious?
오늘 바쁘다고? — You're busy today?

notes.title: Intonation matters
notes.text: With rising intonation, it seeks confirmation. With flat or falling intonation, it can show disbelief.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 297 -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? for 'I heard that...' and ID 298 -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? for expressing stronger disbelief.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? is used when you want to confirm something you heard from someone else. It translates to 'I heard that...' or 'Didn't you say...?' and carries a tone of seeking verification.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다면서(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다면서(요)?.

examples.english: 한국에 간다면서요? — I heard you're going to Korea?
내일 온다면서? — Didn't you say you're coming tomorrow?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다면서(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다면서(요)?.

examples.english: 다이어트한다면서요? — Didn't you say you're on a diet?

rules.title: Adjective → + 다면서(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다면서(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 그 집이 싸다면서요? — I heard that house is cheap?
바쁘다면서요? — Didn't you say you were busy?

notes.title: Confrontational nuance
notes.text: If the facts don't match what you heard, this can sound slightly confrontational. Use 요 to soften.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 296 -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? for echo questions and ID 298 -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? for expressing disbelief.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? expresses surprise or disbelief at what someone just said. It translates to 'You mean...?' or 'I can't believe...' The emotion is stronger than -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? and often shows genuine shock.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다니(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다니(요)?.

examples.english: 혼자 간다니? — You mean you're going alone? I can't believe it.
그 사람이 온다니요? — He's really coming?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다니(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다니(요)?.

examples.english: 그걸 먹는다니! — I can't believe you're eating that!

rules.title: Adjective → + 다니(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다니(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 이렇게 크다니! — I can't believe it's this big!
시험이 어렵다니요? — You mean the exam is hard?

notes.title: Emotional reaction
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는다니 is not neutral. It always carries emotion — surprise, shock, or disbelief. Use it carefully in formal settings.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 296 -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? for milder confirmation and ID 297 -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? for checking hearsay.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다거나 is indirect speech for listing alternatives. It reports that someone mentioned one or more options — 'they said they do this or that.' It is the quoted form of the casual listing connector -(으)ㄴ/는다거나.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다거나
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다거나.

examples.english: 한국에 간다거나 일본에 간다거나 해요 — They say they might go to Korea or Japan.

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다거나
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다거나.

examples.english: 피자를 먹는다거나 치킨을 시킨다거나 — They said they eat pizza or order chicken.

rules.title: Adjective → + 다거나
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다거나 with no variation.

examples.english: 집이 크다거나 마당이 있다거나 — They said the house is big or it has a yard.
날씨가 좋다거나 바다가 있다거나 — They said the weather is nice or there's a beach.

notes.title: Listing pattern
notes.text: This grammar typically lists two or more options, often ending with a vague verb like 하다 to show uncertainty.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–295 for basic indirect speech forms.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다기에 / 다길래 means 'because they said' or 'since I heard that.' It explains why the speaker took action based on what they heard from someone else. -다길래 is more casual and colloquial; -다기에 is slightly more formal.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다기에 / ㄴ다길래
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다기에 or ㄴ다길래.

examples.english: 친구가 온다기에 많이 준비했어요 — Since I heard my friend was coming, I prepared a lot.
손님이 온다길래 방을 청소했어요 — Since I heard guests were coming, I cleaned the room.

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다기에 / 는다길래
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다기에 or 는다길래.

examples.english: 아이가 안 먹는다길래 다른 걸 만들었어요 — Since I heard the child wasn't eating, I made something else.

rules.title: Adjective → + 다기에 / 다길래
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다기에 or 다길래 with no variation.

examples.english: 친구가 아프다기에 병원에 같이 갔어요 — Since I heard my friend was sick, I went to the hospital with her.
날씨가 춥다길래 코트를 입었어요 — Since I heard it was cold, I wore a coat.

notes.title: Causal link
notes.text: Both forms strongly imply that the speaker acted because of what they heard. The result clause is almost always about the speaker's own action.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–295 for basic indirect speech forms, and ID 299 -(으)ㄴ/는다거나 for listing alternatives.
